question: What are the minimum address number height requirements for commercial establishments under Cobb County Code?
ground_truth: Under Section 106-2(b) of the Cobb County Code, commercial establishments must display address numbers at least six inches high with a reflective contrasting background or reflective numbers on a contrasting background. Three-inch numbers are acceptable for exterior suite numbers, and one-inch numbers are acceptable for interior suite numbers and apartment unit doors. Larger figures may be required to ensure legibility from the street. For residences the minimum height is three inches, or one inch when posted on mailboxes adjacent to the street. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Common Code Violations]

question: At what height must a Knox Box be mounted on a building in Cobb County, and which series is most commonly purchased?
ground_truth: According to the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office Knox Box Guide, the Knox Box must be mounted 5 feet (60 inches) from the ground on the exterior of the structure. The 3200 series is the most commonly purchased model. A tamper switch is an option but is not required. The box should be mounted at the location where emergency crews will most likely enter, usually the exterior door nearest the fire alarm annunciator panel or fire sprinkler control room. Once installed, contact CCFMO at 770-528-8310 so the nearest fire station can meet on site to lock the box with business keys. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Knox Box Guide]

question: What is the minimum number of spare sprinkler heads required on premises, and what are the NFPA 25 storage conditions?
ground_truth: Per Section 5.4.1.4 of NFPA 25 (2011 edition), a supply of spare sprinklers never fewer than six shall be maintained on the premises so that any sprinklers that have operated or been damaged can be promptly replaced. Per Section 5.4.1.4.1, spare sprinklers must correspond to the types and temperature ratings of those installed. Per Section 5.4.1.4.2, they must be kept in a cabinet where the temperature will never exceed 100 degrees F. Per Section 5.4.1.6, a special sprinkler wrench (one per type of sprinkler installed) must also be provided in that cabinet. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Common Code Violations / NFPA 25]

question: What are the fire department apparatus access road requirements for buildings under construction per NFPA 241 (2013)?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 241 (2013 edition) Section 7.5.5.1, buildings under construction must be accessible via roadways with an all-weather driving surface of not less than 20 feet unobstructed width, capable of withstanding fire apparatus live loads, and with a minimum vertical clearance of 13 feet 6 inches. Per Section 7.5.5.2, access must be provided at the start and maintained until completion. Per Section 7.5.5.3, dead-end roads over 150 feet must have approved turning provisions for fire apparatus. Per Section 7.5.5.5, the required road width shall not be obstructed in any manner including by parked vehicles. In buildings over one story, at least one lighted stairway in usable condition must be maintained at all times (Section 7.5.6). [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Requirements for Buildings Under Construction]

question: What are the Georgia State Minimum Standard Codes and their editions in effect as of January 1, 2026?
ground_truth: According to the Georgia Department of Community Affairs 2026 Green Sheet, the Georgia State Minimum Standard Codes effective January 1, 2026 include: International Building Code (2024 Edition), International Residential Code (2024 Edition), International Plumbing Code (2024 Edition), International Mechanical Code (2024 Edition), International Fuel Gas Code (2024 Edition), International Energy Conservation Code (2015 Edition), International Fire Code (2024 Edition), National Electrical Code (2023 Edition), and International Swimming Pool and Spa Code (2024 Edition). The DCA Board omitted the plumbing, electrical, and energy requirements of the IRC for one- and two-family dwellings, so IPC, NEC, and IECC govern those elements instead. Optional codes for local adoption include the International Property Maintenance Code (2018), International Existing Building Code (2018), and National Green Building Standard (2008). [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: 2026 Green Sheet]

question: What minimum insurance liability is required to obtain a development blasting permit in Cobb County, and what seven documents must be submitted?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office Development Blasting Permit Application (Rev. 05/07/2025), applicants must submit current insurance with a minimum liability of $1,000,000.00. The seven required documents are: (1) Current State of Georgia Explosive License, (2) Current Federal Explosive License, (3) Current business license within the State of Georgia, (4) Current insurance showing $1,000,000 liability, (5) Current copy of the blasters blasting license with picture, (6) Site map showing legible street names of the blasting location, (7) Current copy of the blasters driver license. If blasting occurs within 2,000 feet of structures or facilities, a Pre-Blast assessment must be maintained. Prior Bomb Squad approval is required before the Fire Marshal Office issues the permit. Permits are limited to 30 days maximum. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Development Blasting Permit Application]

question: What are the AED installation requirements for commercial buildings in Cobb County per the Code of Ordinances (Sec. 54-65)?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-65, AEDs shall be required and installed by the property owner or occupant in all commercial businesses and buildings that are open to the public, located within unincorporated Cobb County, and have an occupancy load of 300 or more as defined and established by the Cobb County fire marshal. The installation and maintenance of AED(s) is the responsibility of the owner and occupant prior to the issuance of each certificate of occupancy. The fire department or fire marshal shall verify AED installation prior to the issuance of a certificate of occupancy. Owners and occupants must comply with all applicable state laws including O.C.G.A. Section 31-11-53.2 governing installation and use of AEDs. The fire marshal may conduct regular inspections to confirm AED availability after installation. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-65]

question: What charcoal grilling restrictions apply on combustible balconies under Cobb County fire code, and what three exceptions apply?
ground_truth: Per Section 308.1.4 of the 2012 International Fire Code (listed as Common Code Violation #15 in Cobb County), charcoal burners and other open-flame cooking devices shall not be operated on combustible balconies or within 10 feet of combustible construction. The three exceptions are: (1) One- and two-family dwellings, (2) where buildings, balconies, and decks are protected by an automatic sprinkler system, and (3) LP-gas cooking devices having an LP-gas container with a water capacity no greater than 2.5 pounds. This violation is actively inspected by the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office at existing apartment occupancies. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Common Code Violations]

question: How is fire code authority structured in Georgia, and what three duties does the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office perform per O.C.G.A.?
ground_truth: Fire code authority flows from state to local level. Title 25, Chapter 2 of the O.C.G.A. authorizes the Safety Fire Commissioner to adopt rules under Chapter 120-3-3 (Rules and Regulations for the State Minimum Fire Safety Standards), which include certain NFPA standards and the Standard Fire Prevention Code. Per Section 25-2-4 O.C.G.A., these codes have the force and effect of law. Cobb County adopts these state minimum standards by reference in Cobb County Code 54-54. Per Cobb County Ordinances Sec. 54-56, wherever the term fire code official appears in the International Fire Code, it means the fire marshal, and wherever authority having jurisdiction is used, it also means the fire marshal. Per Section 25-2-12 O.C.G.A., the CCFMO performs three duties: (1) conducting fire safety inspections of existing buildings and structures, (2) reviewing plans for proposed buildings, and (3) issuing permanent and temporary certificates of occupancy. CCFMO does NOT issue Temporary Certificates of Occupancy; the Building Department issues the CO after FMO release. [TYPE: reasoning | SOURCE: Common Code Violations + Cobb County Ordinances Sec. 54-55/56]

question: Is a building owner permitted to turn off fire sprinkler water supply during freezing weather and post a fire watch instead?
ground_truth: No, this is not permissible. CCFMO Code Application Policy #5-1 (Freezing Weather and Fire Sprinkler Systems) explicitly states that pre-emptive disabling of a sprinkler system during freezing weather is inconsistent with the State of Georgia Minimum Fire Safety Standards and is not an approved use of a fire watch. Per Section 901.8 of the 2012 International Fire Code (adopted by Georgia and Cobb County), it is unlawful for any person to remove, tamper with, or otherwise disturb any fire suppression system except for fire extinguishment, training, recharging, necessary repairs, or when approved by the fire code official. Fire sprinkler systems must remain active and operational at all times when the building or space is occupied. Wet pipe sprinkler systems must be kept above 40 degrees F. Building owners who disable or cause disabling of sprinkler systems take on significant personal and professional liability, and the act is punishable by law. [TYPE: reasoning | SOURCE: Freezing Weather Policy + Common Code Violations]

question: What must happen immediately when a required fire protection system goes out of service in Cobb County, and what are the specific duties of a fire watch?
ground_truth: Per Section 901.7 of the 2012 International Fire Code, when a required fire protection system is out of service, the fire department and fire code official must be notified immediately. The fire code official may then require the building to be evacuated or an approved fire watch provided for all unprotected occupants until the system is restored. Where fire watches are utilized they must: (1) be provided with at least one approved means for notifying the fire department, and (2) have no other duty than to perform constant patrols of the protected premises and watch for fires. Per Cobb County Code Sec. 54-62, a fire watch is a temporary measure intended to ensure continuous and systematic surveillance of a building by one or more qualified individuals for the purposes of identifying and controlling fire hazards, detecting early signs of unwanted fire, and raising an alarm. Pre-emptive disabling of a sprinkler system does not constitute an approved use of a fire watch per CCFMO Policy #5-1. [TYPE: reasoning | SOURCE: Common Code Violations + Cobb County Ordinances Sec. 54-62]

question: What type of Knox product is required for electric gates versus manual gates restricting emergency vehicle access, and what key storage rules apply?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office Knox Box Guide and Pre-Construction Meeting checklist (item 10), the required Knox product differs by gate type: Electric (mechanically operated) gates require a Knox Switch, while manual gates may use a Knox Padlock. This requirement is grounded in Section 506.1.1 of the International Fire Code, as adopted by Cobb County Code 54-54, which requires an approved lock on gates or similar barriers when required by the fire code official. Keys shall be stored in the Knox box providing access to the structure or occupancy. Magnetic cards and keypad codes are NOT acceptable substitutes for emergency building entry. Order at www.knoxbox.com using zip code 30008 and select Cobb Co Fire and Emerg Svcs. [TYPE: reasoning | SOURCE: Knox Box Guide + Pre-Construction Meeting]

question: What are the complete steps for a tenant in Unincorporated Cobb County to obtain a Certificate of Occupancy, and how does approval expiration affect the timeline per the Ordinances?
ground_truth: Per the 2026 Tenant Space CO Procedures (revised 1/20/2026): (1) Start at Cobb County Community Development; upload Commercial Permit Application, one set of drawings/floorplans, and scope of work into Accela to receive an application number. (2) Submit a plan review appointment via Mobile-eyes.com at cobbcounty.gov/fmo. Required uploads include: Commercial Permit Application with CCWS signature on page 2, one set of plans (dimensions, room labels, door/window/fixture locations, emergency lighting, exit signs, fire extinguishers, tenant separation walls, fire rated barriers, sprinkler/alarm notation, and mall walkway dimensions if applicable), scope of work letter, Occupant/Owners Information Certificate, and a key plan. (3) After FMO approval, schedule inspections via Mobile-eyes. (4) After all inspections pass, email subpermits@cobbcounty.gov or call 770-528-2071 for the CO; allow up to 48 hours. Per Cobb County Ordinances Sec. 54-51.1, FMO approvals expire if work is not commenced within 180 days of issuance, or if a permit is not issued by the building official within 90 days of Fire Marshal approval. Cities of Marietta, Smyrna, and Austell have their own Fire Marshal offices. [TYPE: reasoning | SOURCE: CO Procedures + Cobb County Ordinances Sec. 54-51.1]

question: What are all the rules governing reinspection fees in Cobb County per the Code of Ordinances, including when fees are assessed, payment deadlines, and the appeal process?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-53, reinspection fees are tiered: First reinspection $50.00; Second reinspection $100.00; Third reinspection and every subsequent reinspection $150.00. Key rules: (1) Fees are assessed upon COMPLETION of a scheduled reinspection when the cited code violation(s) has not been corrected. (2) If a NEW violation is discovered at reinspection that was not part of the original violation list, no fee is assessed for that new violation; it is treated as a first-time violation and restarts the reinspection fee process. (3) Reinspection fees must be paid within 30 days of the reinspection date to Cobb County Fire and Emergency Services. (4) No fire safety code release or certificate of occupancy will be issued until all reinspection fees are paid in full. (5) Disputed fees may be appealed in writing to the fire marshal office, with final authority resting with the fire marshal. (6) Reinspection fees also apply to plan review appointments not cancelled at least 24 hours prior to the scheduled review time. The inspector will notify the responsible party (contractor, owner, or manager) when a fee is assessed, or post a citation on site. [TYPE: reasoning | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-53]

question: Under NFPA 241, what are the standpipe requirements for buildings under construction, and when must temporary standpipes be installed per Cobb County requirements?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 241 (2013 edition) Section 7.6, standpipes in new buildings or buildings being altered/demolished must be maintained in conformity with construction progress and always ready for use. Per Section 8.7.4.2.1, standpipes must have conspicuously marked FDC connections at street level and at least one standard hose outlet per floor. Per Section 8.7.4.2.8, standpipes must be extended up with each floor and capped at the top. Per Section 8.7.4.2.9, top hose outlets must not be more than one floor below the highest forms, staging, and combustibles. Per Section 8.7.4.2.10, temporary standpipes remain in service until the permanent installation is complete. Per the Cobb County Pre-Construction Meeting (item 26), a temporary standpipe is required when a building is 4 or more stories and before it reaches 40 feet in height above the lowest level of fire department access. Automatic standpipes require a roof flow test of 500 GPM at 100 PSI at the most remote outlet; pressure reducing valves are required over 175 PSI and set to a maximum of 175 PSI. [TYPE: reasoning | SOURCE: Requirements for Buildings Under Construction + Pre-Construction Meeting]

question: What are the complete Knox Box installation requirements in Cobb County, combining the Knox Box Guide and the Pre-Construction Meeting checklist?
ground_truth: From the Knox Box Guide and Pre-Construction Meeting combined: Required locations: (1) Structures/occupancies with fire sprinkler systems, (2) structures/occupancies with fire alarm systems, (3) gates limiting emergency vehicle access, (4) other CCFMO-designated locations. Product: Order at www.knoxbox.com using zip code 30008, select Cobb Co Fire and Emerg Svcs; 3200 series is most common. Gate products: electric/mechanical gates need a Knox Switch; manual gates need a Knox Padlock. Mounting: Outside the structure at the most likely emergency entry point (nearest the fire alarm annunciator panel or sprinkler control room), at 5 feet (60 inches) from the ground. Activation: After installation, call CCFMO at 770-528-8310; the nearest fire station meets on site to lock the box with business keys. Restriction: Magnetic cards and keypad codes are NOT acceptable for emergency building entry. Code authority: Section 506 of the International Fire Code, as adopted by Cobb County Code 54-54. [TYPE: multi-context | SOURCE: Knox Box Guide + Pre-Construction Meeting]

question: What documentation must be collected BEFORE scheduling a fire sprinkler final inspection AND a fire alarm final inspection in Cobb County per the Pre-Construction Meeting?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Pre-Construction Meeting: For a sprinkler final inspection (item 28), ALL of the following must be in hand before scheduling: above-ground test certificates, compliance letters, site supervision forms, third-party inspections (e.g., attic insulation reports), and all painting operations must be complete. For welded pipe (item 21), the welders mark must appear on the pipe OR a welders certificate must be provided prior to inspection. For a fire alarm final inspection (item 33), the following must be collected prior to scheduling: NFPA 72 Record of Completion, Inspection and Testing form; decibel levels letter; copy of NICET/CFAT certification; and an activity report. Per item 31, a NICET or CFAT certified technician must be on site for all fire alarm system testing. The recommended sequence (item 32): schedule a device-check inspection first to verify installation height, location, and settings; then schedule the final inspection only after devices are verified and the system is fully operational. [TYPE: multi-context | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting]

question: What are the wet pipe sprinkler temperature and heating requirements across the Pre-Construction Meeting and Freezing Weather policy, and what are the legal consequences of disabling a sprinkler to prevent freeze damage?
ground_truth: From the Pre-Construction Meeting and Freezing Weather and Fire Sprinkler Systems policy combined: (1) Temperature requirement: All wet sprinkler pipes must maintain a minimum of 40 degrees Fahrenheit. (2) Heating methods: All heating methods must be listed, direct wired, and thermostatically controlled; heat trace must be listed and supervised (Pre-Construction Meeting item 23). (3) Common freeze risks: In NFPA 13R systems, inadequate attic insulation is a frequent problem; R30-Value insulation thickness is recommended; sprinkler control valve boxes/rooms must be inspected for proper insulation and heating. In NFPA 13 systems, the drum drip on dry systems is often improperly maintained. Unoccupied spaces with no active heating can also cause wet pipe freezing. (4) Legal consequences: Per Section 901.8 of the 2012 International Fire Code, disabling a fire suppression system is unlawful and punishable by law unless for fire extinguishment, training, recharging, necessary repairs, or with fire code official approval. Building owners take on significant personal and professional liability. (5) A properly maintained and insulated fire sprinkler system should not suffer freeze damage. [TYPE: multi-context | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting + Freezing Weather Policy]

question: What fire safety site requirements must be met before combustible materials arrive on a new commercial construction site in Cobb County, drawing from the Pre-Construction Meeting, NFPA 241, and the Cobb County Code of Ordinances?
ground_truth: From the Pre-Construction Meeting, NFPA 241, and Cobb County Ordinances combined: (1) All private hydrants must be commissioned before combustible materials arrive (Pre-Construction Meeting item 12), implementing NFPA 241 Sec. 8.7.2.1 (water supply required as soon as combustible material accumulates). Hydrants must be painted silver with CCFMO-approved orange reflective tape, 18-inch minimum outlet height, and 36-inch clearance; a piping flush witnessed by CCFMO and a flow test for all new hydrants are required. Per Cobb County Ordinances Sec. 54-59, hydrant maintenance records must be kept on premises. (2) Fire department access must meet NFPA 241 standards: 20-foot minimum all-weather road, fire apparatus load capacity, 13-foot 6-inch vertical clearance, turning provisions for dead-ends over 150 feet (Pre-Construction Meeting item 7). (3) Address numbers minimum 6 inches, legible from street, must be posted; wayfinding signage required if needed (item 8). Per Cobb County Ordinances Sec. 54-63, additional directional signage is required for multi-building developments. (4) A Knox Box at 60 inches is required for any building with a fire alarm or sprinkler system; Knox switches/padlocks required for gated access (item 10). Underground pipe must not be covered until CCFMO approval; minimum burial depth is 42 inches. [TYPE: multi-context | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting + NFPA 241 + Cobb County Ordinances Sec. 54-59, 54-63]

question: What minimum working space must be maintained around fire department connections on existing buildings per Section 912.3.2 of the 2012 International Fire Code?
ground_truth: Per Section 912.3.2 of the 2012 International Fire Code, a working space of not less than 36 inches in width, 36 inches in depth, and 78 inches in height shall be provided and maintained in front of and to the sides of wall-mounted fire department connections, and around the circumference of free-standing fire department connections, except as otherwise required or approved by the fire chief. Per Section 912.3, immediate access to fire department connections shall be maintained at all times without obstruction by fences, bushes, trees, walls, or any other object. This is enforced as Common Code Violation #4 (FIRE DEPARTMENT CONNECTION NOT VISIBLE TO APPARATUS OR OBSTRUCTED) by the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Common Code Violations / 2012 IFC Sec. 912.3, 912.3.2]

question: What does Section 605.6 of the 2012 International Fire Code require regarding electrical junction boxes and wiring splices?
ground_truth: Per Section 605.6 of the 2012 International Fire Code, open junction boxes and open-wiring splices shall be prohibited. Approved covers shall be provided for all switch and electrical outlet boxes. This is enforced by the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office as Common Code Violation #2 (EXPOSED WIRING). The Existing Apartment Occupancy Checklist also specifically includes inspection of electrical junction boxes being covered as a required pass/fail item. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Common Code Violations / 2012 IFC Sec. 605.6]

question: What are the working space dimensions required in front of electrical service equipment per Section 605.3 of the 2012 International Fire Code, and what storage restriction applies?
ground_truth: Per Section 605.3 of the 2012 International Fire Code, a working space of not less than 30 inches in width, 36 inches in depth, and 78 inches in height shall be provided in front of electrical service equipment. Where the electrical service equipment is wider than 30 inches, the working space shall not be less than the width of the equipment. No storage of any materials shall be located within the designated working space. This is enforced by CCFMO as Common Code Violation #6 (STORAGE WITHIN 30 INCHES OF ELECTRICAL PANELS) and is a required inspection item on the Existing Apartment Occupancy Checklist. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Common Code Violations / 2012 IFC Sec. 605.3]

question: What restrictions does Section 605.5 of the 2012 International Fire Code place on extension cord and flexible cord use?
ground_truth: Per Section 605.5 of the 2012 International Fire Code, extension cords and flexible cords shall not be a substitute for permanent wiring. Extension cords and flexible cords shall not be affixed to structures, extended through walls, ceilings or floors, or under doors or floor coverings, nor shall such cords be subject to environmental damage or physical impact. Extension cords shall be used only with portable appliances. This is enforced as Common Code Violation #7 (EXTENSION CORDS USED AS PERMANENT WIRING) and is a required pass/fail inspection item on the Existing Apartment Occupancy Checklist. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Common Code Violations / 2012 IFC Sec. 605.5]

question: What clearance distances must be maintained from storage to sprinkler heads per NFPA 25 Section A-5.2.1.2, and what correction does the code require when obstructions exist?
ground_truth: Per Section 5.2.1.2 of NFPA 25 (2011 edition), unacceptable obstructions to spray patterns shall be corrected. Per Section A-5.2.1.2, NFPA 13 allows stock, furnishings, and equipment to be as close as 18 inches to standard spray sprinklers, or as close as 36 inches to ESFR (Early Suppression Fast Response) and large drop sprinklers. Any storage that violates these clearances constitutes Common Code Violation #11 (SPRINKLERS BLOCKED BY STORAGE). When obstructions are found, they must be removed or relocated to restore the required clearances to sprinkler heads. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Common Code Violations / NFPA 25 Sec. 5.2.1.2]

question: What does NFPA 10 Section 7.3.2.1 require for annual fire extinguisher inspections, and what must each extinguisher have attached after maintenance?
ground_truth: Per Section 7.3.2.1 of NFPA 10 (2013 edition), an annual external visual examination of all fire extinguishers shall be made to detect obvious physical damage, corrosion, or nozzle blockage; to verify that operating instructions are present, legible, and facing forward; to verify that HMIS information is present and legible; and to determine if a 6-year interval examination or hydrostatic test is due. Per Section 7.3.4.1, each extinguisher shall have a tag or label securely attached indicating that maintenance was performed. Failure to have current maintenance constitutes Common Code Violation #12 (EXTINGUISHERS OUT OF DATE). [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Common Code Violations / NFPA 10 Sec. 7.3.2.1, 7.3.4.1]

question: What does Section 5303.5.3 of the 2012 International Fire Code require for compressed gas cylinders, and what three causes of falling must securing prevent?
ground_truth: Per Section 5303.5.3 of the 2012 International Fire Code, compressed gas containers, cylinders, and tanks shall be secured to prevent falling caused by: (1) contact, (2) vibration, or (3) seismic activity. Failure to secure cylinders constitutes Common Code Violation #5 (COMPRESSED GAS CYLINDER NOT SECURED) and is also a required pass/fail inspection item on the Existing Apartment Occupancy Checklist under the Miscellaneous section. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Common Code Violations / 2012 IFC Sec. 5303.5.3]

question: What 14 systems or structures require separate plan submittals to the CCFMO per the Pre-Construction Meeting?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office Pre-Construction Meeting (item 3), separate plan submittals are required for the following 14 items: (a) Fire alarm, (b) Sprinkler systems, (c) NFPA 96 hood systems, (d) Special locking arrangements, (e) Paint booths, (f) Clean agent systems, (g) Bleachers/Grandstands, (h) Tanks, (i) Fire pumps, (j) IFC 510 systems, (k) Underground fire main, (l) Racks, (m) Parking structure ventilation systems, and (n) Stairwell pressurization. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting]

question: What are the height, setback, and operational requirements for Post Indicator Valves (PIVs) per the Cobb County Pre-Construction Meeting?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office Pre-Construction Meeting (item 25), PIVs must be located 40 feet from the structure, on the supply side of the FDC, and must be locked, tampered, and targeted. Height must be 32 to 40 inches above final grade. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting]

question: What are the handrail height range, clearance from wall, and guardrail height requirements per the Cobb County Pre-Construction Meeting?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office Pre-Construction Meeting (item 34), handrails must be installed between 34 and 38 inches in height with a minimum clearance of 2 and 1/4 inches from the wall. Guardrails must be a minimum of 42 inches in height. Stairs less than 21 inches in elevation require 13-inch treads. Temporary stairs are required for multi-story buildings. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting]

question: What door hardware requirements for egress compliance does the Cobb County Pre-Construction Meeting specify?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office Pre-Construction Meeting (item 37), all door hardware requires a single action to release. If not panic hardware, it must be a lever style. Privacy latches for hotels and motels must be installed at or below 48 inches. No keyed locks are allowed in the path of egress. Contractors must schedule an inspection to view one locking arrangement. If using spring-loaded hinges for apartment unit doors, there must be at least three spring-loaded hinges. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting]

question: What HVAC CFM thresholds determine duct detector placement requirements, and what reporting is required if the building has a fire alarm system?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office Pre-Construction Meeting (item 43), an HVAC smoke shutdown test is required on all units over 2,000 CFM. The duct detector must be on the supply side of the unit. If the unit is over 15,000 CFM, detectors are required on both the supply and return sides. If the building has a fire alarm system, the HVAC shutdown must report as a supervisory signal. If no fire alarm system is present, a remote audio/visual annunciator must be installed in a normally occupied area. Live smoke must be used for the test. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting]

question: What are the minimum setback distances for transformer pads from a building and from doorways per the Cobb County Pre-Construction Meeting?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office Pre-Construction Meeting (item 11), transformer pad edges must be a minimum of 10 feet from the structure, overhang, exterior stairs or walkways connected to the building, windows, or other openings. The minimum setback from doorways is 14 feet. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting]

question: What is the minimum burial depth for underground fire mains, and what must happen before any pipe is covered per the Cobb County Pre-Construction Meeting?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Pre-Construction Meeting (item 14), the minimum burial depth for underground fire main piping is 42 inches. Pipe must not be covered until approved by the CCFMO. If a contractor covers pipe without CCFMO approval, they will be required to expose all piping for inspection. The maximum riser height from underground into the building is 10 feet. Items inspected include pipe size, restraints, thrust blocks, and deflection. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting]

question: What are the permitted days and hours for blasting activity under a Cobb County development blasting permit per Cobb County Code Sec. 18-361?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code Sec. 18-361(d)(2), permitted hours of blasting activity are 10:00 a.m. through 4:00 p.m., Monday through Friday. Blasting is not permitted on weekends, state holidays, or federal holidays. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Development Blasting Ordinance Sec. 18-361]

question: What are the blasting vibration-frequency and air blast intensity limits under Cobb County Code Sec. 18-360?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code Sec. 18-360(b), blast intensity may not exceed the following limits: (1) Blasting vibration-frequency limits: The velocity/shock wave of any blast shall not exceed 2 inches per second PPV at 40 Hz or greater. At lower frequencies, the established limits in U.S. Bureau of Mines RI 8507 apply, as amended. (2) Air blast limits: 140 decibels or as defined by the U.S. Bureau of Mines RI 8485, as may be amended. The director has discretion to require monitoring at alternate locations if warranted by complaints. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Development Blasting Ordinance Sec. 18-360]

question: What escrow amount may be required from a development blasting permit applicant in Cobb County, and what is its purpose per Cobb County Code Sec. 18-361?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code Sec. 18-361(d)(12), the applicant may be required to execute an escrow agreement and fund a cash escrow account in the amount of $50,000.00 prior to issuance of the blasting permit. The sole purpose of this escrow agreement is to compensate property owners for cosmetic or structural damage to their property resulting from the blasting activity. Cobb County serves as the escrow agent. Disbursements are based on the decision of an arbiter through binding arbitration. The applicant is solely responsible for the costs of the arbitration proceedings. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Development Blasting Ordinance Sec. 18-361]

question: What are the penalties for a first intensity blasting violation under Cobb County Code Sec. 18-363, including fines and work stoppage?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code Sec. 18-363(a)(1), a first intensity violation results in: (a) a fine of $500.00 against the user, blaster, developer, and engineer, who are jointly liable for the full amount, and/or potential revocation of the blasting permit based on the nature of the violation and compliance history; and (b) all work under the blasting permit must cease for seven days. Blasting may resume only after the user obtains approval for a modification of the blasting permit ensuring that intensity will not exceed the limits in Sec. 18-361, based on an investigation report outlining remedial alternatives. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Development Blasting Ordinance Sec. 18-363]

question: How many exits are required for buildings with 500–1,000 occupants and for buildings exceeding 1,000 occupants per NFPA 101 as used in the CCFMO New Shell Building Plan Review?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 101 Section 7.4.1.2, as referenced in the Cobb County New Building Plan Review checklist (item 12), occupant loads between 500 and 1,000 must provide a minimum of 3 exits. Occupant loads of 1,000 or more must provide a minimum of 4 exits. For occupant loads below 500, a minimum of 2 means of egress are required from any balcony, mezzanine, story, or portion thereof per NFPA 101 Section 7.4.1.1. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: New Shell Building Plan Review 2024 / NFPA 101 Sec. 7.4.1.2]

question: What is the minimum clear door width required per NFPA 101 Section 7.2.1.2.3.2 in the Cobb County New Shell Building Plan Review checklist?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 101 Section 7.2.1.2.3.2, as referenced in the Cobb County New Building Plan Review checklist (item 15), doors shall provide not less than 32 inches of clear width. In general, the minimum egress width shall not be less than 36 inches per NFPA 101 Section 7.3.4.1 unless modified by the applicable occupancy chapter (checklist item 11). [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: New Shell Building Plan Review 2024 / NFPA 101 Sec. 7.2.1.2.3.2]

question: What is the maximum allowable threshold height at commercial door openings per NFPA 101 Section 7.2.1.3.3?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 101 Section 7.2.1.3.3, as referenced in the Cobb County New Building Plan Review checklist (item 16), thresholds at door openings shall not be greater than 1/2 inch. This requirement is confirmed in the Cobb County Pre-Construction Meeting (item 36), which specifies that commercial thresholds are required to be 1/2 inch maximum. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: New Shell Building Plan Review 2024 + Pre-Construction Meeting / NFPA 101 Sec. 7.2.1.3.3]

question: When does NFPA 1124 require a fire sprinkler system in a Consumer Fireworks Retail Sales (CFRS) facility?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 1124 Section 7.3.6, as referenced in the Cobb County CFRS Facilities Plan Review checklist (item 8), sprinklers are required in CFRS facilities where the gross weight of consumer fireworks exceeds 500 pounds. The plan review checklist also requires the applicant to indicate whether the fireworks inventory is less than 125 lbs (500 lbs gross), greater than 125 lbs (500 lbs gross), or greater than 250 lbs (1,000 lbs gross). [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Consumer Fireworks Retail Sales (CFRS) Facilities 2024 / NFPA 1124 Sec. 7.3.6]

question: What are the minimum exit count, maximum travel distance, and aisle requirements for Consumer Fireworks Retail Sales facilities per NFPA 1124?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 1124 as referenced in the Cobb County CFRS Facilities Plan Review checklist: a minimum of 3 exits must be provided (item 20, NFPA 1124 7.3.14.1.1); exits must not pass through storage areas (item 21, NFPA 1124 7.3.14.1.2); exit travel distance must not exceed 75 feet (item 18, NFPA 1124 7.3.14.2); aisles must be a minimum of 48 inches wide (item 22a, NFPA 1124 7.3.14.3); dead-end aisles are not permitted; at least one aisle must lead directly to an exit; and other required exits must be within 10 feet of the end of an aisle or cross aisle. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Consumer Fireworks Retail Sales (CFRS) Facilities 2024 / NFPA 1124]

question: What separation distances must a Consumer Fireworks Retail Sales facility maintain from propane-dispensing stations and aboveground bulk storage per NFPA 1124?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 1124 as referenced in the Cobb County CFRS Facilities Plan Review checklist: CFRS facilities must maintain 50 feet from propane-dispensing station dispensers (NFPA 1124 7.3.12.1(1)); 50 feet from aboveground storage tanks for flammable or combustible liquids, gas, or liquefied gas (7.3.12.1(2)); 50 feet from compressed natural gas-dispensing station dispensers (7.3.12.1(3)); 50 feet from motor vehicle fuel-dispensing station dispensers (7.3.12.2); and a minimum of 300 feet from any aboveground bulk storage or bulk dispensing area for flammable or combustible liquid, gas, or liquefied gas (7.3.12.6). [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Consumer Fireworks Retail Sales (CFRS) Facilities 2024 / NFPA 1124]

question: What fire extinguisher types, hazard ratings, and minimum counts are required for a CFRS facility with electrical power per NFPA 1124?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 1124 as referenced in the Cobb County CFRS Facilities Plan Review checklist: every CFRS facility shall have no fewer than two extinguishers (item 14, NFPA 1124 7.3.6.3), with at least one being a pressurized water type and the other being a multipurpose dry chemical type if the facility is provided with electrical power (NFPA 1124 7.3.8.2). All portable fire extinguishers must be rated for extra (high) hazard occupancy (item 13, NFPA 1124 7.3.8.1). The maximum travel distance to an extinguisher is 75 feet (item 12, NFPA 1124 7.3.8.3). [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Consumer Fireworks Retail Sales (CFRS) Facilities 2024 / NFPA 1124]

question: What signage must be posted on the exterior of a CFRS facility and within aisles per NFPA 1124?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 1124 as referenced in the Cobb County CFRS Facilities Plan Review checklist: A sign with minimum 4-inch high letters on a contrasting background must be posted on each side of the exterior of the CFRS facility reading: 'NO FIREWORKS DISCHARGE WITHIN 300 FEET' (item 40, NFPA 1124 7.4.6.3). Additionally, a sign with minimum 2-inch high letters on a contrasting background must be posted within 10 feet of every aisle serving CFRS areas reading: 'FIREWORKS – NO SMOKING WITHIN 50 FEET' (item 41, NFPA 1124 7.3.11). [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Consumer Fireworks Retail Sales (CFRS) Facilities 2024 / NFPA 1124]

question: What maximum merchandise display heights apply in CFRS facilities, and how much floor space must remain open per NFPA 1124?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 1124 as referenced in the Cobb County CFRS Facilities Plan Review checklist: General merchandise displays must not exceed 6 feet in height (item 33, NFPA 1124 7.3.15.2). Merchandise displays along the perimeter of the retail sales area may be a maximum of 12 feet in height (item 34, NFPA 1124 7.3.15.2.2). No display shall be located within 5 feet of a public entrance or 2 feet of a private entrance (item 38, NFPA 1124 7.3.19.2). Not less than 50% of the available floor area within the retail area must be open space unoccupied by displays (item 39, NFPA 1124 7.3.20). [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Consumer Fireworks Retail Sales (CFRS) Facilities 2024 / NFPA 1124]

question: What standby power transfer time and continuous duration requirements apply to high-rise buildings per NFPA 101 and IFC?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County High-Rise Building Plan Review checklist: Standby power must provide a 60-second transfer time (item 19a, NFPA 101 11.8.5.2.1) and the ability to provide continuous power for 120 minutes (item 19b, IFC 604.2.1.14.1.1). Emergency power (a separate system) must transfer within 10 seconds (item 18, IFC 604.2.14.3 and NFPA 101 7.9.1.3) and must support exit signs, exit illumination, electric fire pumps, and elevator car lighting. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: High-Rise Checklist 2024 / NFPA 101 Sec. 11.8.5.2.1, IFC 604.2.14.3]

question: What seven electrical systems must high-rise standby power serve simultaneously per IFC 604.2.14.1.2?
ground_truth: Per IFC Section 604.2.14.1.2 as referenced in the Cobb County High-Rise Building Plan Review checklist (item 19c), standby power must provide electrical capacity for all of the following to run simultaneously: (1) Fire pump, (2) Jockey pump, (3) Air compressors for dry systems or pre-action systems, (4) Emergency command center equipment and lights, (5) One elevator serving all floors with transfer ability to any other elevator, (6) Mechanical equipment required to operate smokeproof enclosures, and (7) Mechanical equipment required to operate the smoke control system. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: High-Rise Checklist 2024 / IFC 604.2.14.1.2]

question: What stair signage letter size, placement height, and content requirements apply to high-rise buildings in Cobb County per NFPA 101 Section 7.2.2.5.4?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 101 Section 7.2.2.5.4 as referenced in the Cobb County High-Rise Building Plan Review checklist (item 6), stairway signage must include: stair identifier, floor level, level of exit discharge, and direction to exit discharge on each floor landing. Signage must detail terminus at the top and bottom of the stair enclosure and must indicate no roof access. Signage must be located approximately 60 inches above the floor landing and visible when the door is open or closed. Letter height minimum is 1 inch; the floor level number must be minimum 5 inches high in the center of the sign. Mezzanines are designated with 'M' and basements with 'B'. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: High-Rise Checklist 2024 / NFPA 101 Sec. 7.2.2.5.4]

question: What 11 items plus smoke control panel must be present in a high-rise Emergency Command Center per NFPA 101 Sec. 11.8.6.2?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 101 Section 11.8.6.2 as referenced in the Cobb County High-Rise Building Plan Review checklist (items 20–31), the Emergency Command Center must contain: (1) Voice fire alarm system panels and controls, (2) Two-way communication service panels and controls, (3) Fire detection and fire alarm annunciation panels, (4) Elevator floor location and operation annunciators, (5) Elevator Recall switch, (6) Elevator emergency power selector switch(es), (7) Sprinkler Valve and waterflow annunciators, (8) Emergency generator status indicators, (9) Stairway door unlocking system controls, (10) Fire pump status indicators, (11) Telephone for fire department use with controlled access to the public telephone system, and a Smoke Control/Smoke Removal graphics panel (item 31). [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: High-Rise Checklist 2024 / NFPA 101 Sec. 11.8.6.2]

question: What does the Cobb County Pre-Construction Meeting specify for new generator installations, including transfer test and tank permit requirements?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office Pre-Construction Meeting (item 40), if a new generator is installed, a transfer test is required with a maximum 10-second transfer time. All acceptance testing documents and load bank test documents must be provided. Signage is required for all natural gas valves. A tank permit is required for all diesel tanks over 660 gallons, and state approval is also required for those tanks. Generators and their associated tanks must be permitted separately (New Shell Building Plan Review item 47). [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting + New Shell Building Plan Review 2024]

question: What inspection requirements apply to two-hour and above fire-rated barriers during construction per the Cobb County Pre-Construction Meeting?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office Pre-Construction Meeting (item 42), all two-hour and above rated barriers are inspected by the CCFMO, excluding exterior walls or structures required to be fire-resistant rated construction by the IBC. Multi-layer systems require each layer to be inspected prior to covering. Failure to comply requires removal of the covering layer(s). UL details or equivalent are required, including head-of-wall details and fire stopping. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting]

question: How does the tenant Certificate of Occupancy process differ for businesses in unincorporated Cobb County versus those in Kennesaw, Acworth, Mableton, or Powder Springs?
ground_truth: Per the 2026 Tenant Space CO Procedures: For businesses in unincorporated Cobb County, the process must begin at Cobb County Community Development by uploading a commercial permit application, one set of drawings, and scope of work into Accela to receive an application number before proceeding to the FMO website for a plan review appointment via Mobile-eyes. For businesses in Kennesaw, Acworth, Mableton, or Powder Springs, Fire Marshal review begins directly in Mobile-eyes.com without the Accela step. Both pathways require a Commercial Permit Application with the Cobb County Water Department (CCWS) signature on page 2, obtained by emailing CCWSplanreview@cobbcounty.gov. The cities of Marietta, Smyrna, and Austell have their own Fire Marshal offices. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Tenant CO Procedures 2026]

question: What minimum pedestrian walkway width is required in front of each tenant space in a mall per the Cobb County Tenant CO Procedures?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County 2026 Tenant Space Certificate of Occupancy Procedures (plan requirements, item 2), tenant spaces in a mall must show the pedestrian walkway in front of the tenant space. A minimum of 10 feet clear width is required in front of each tenant, including any temporary construction walls. The mall space overall must provide a minimum of 20 feet clear width. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Tenant CO Procedures 2026]

question: What authority does Cobb County Fire and Emergency Services have over private fire hydrants at one-family and two-family dwellings per Cobb County Code Sec. 54-59(a)?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code Sec. 54-59(a), for private fire hydrants located at one-family and two-family dwellings, including townhouses and condominiums, Cobb County Fire and Emergency Services has the authority to enforce all codes with regard to fire hydrants, including but not limited to: location, inspections, maintenance, flow tests (every three years), and obstructions. These private hydrants must comply with applicable codes, standards, and local ordinances currently adopted by Cobb County, enforceable per the terms of Sec. 54-51. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Hydrant Ordinance Sec. 54-59]

question: What fire barrier requirements apply to CFRS storage rooms and multi-tenant CFRS spaces per NFPA 1124?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 1124 as referenced in the Cobb County CFRS Facilities Plan Review checklist: storage rooms containing consumer fireworks must either be sprinklered or separated from the retail area by 1-hour fire barriers (item 16, NFPA 1124 7.5.3). Doors between the storage room and retail area must be self-closing and rated not less than 1 hour (item 17, NFPA 1124 7.5.3.1). For spaces in multiple-tenant buildings, 2-hour fire barrier demising walls are required; however, 1-hour fire barrier walls are acceptable when the space is provided with sprinklers (item 43, NFPA 1124 7.4.4). [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Consumer Fireworks Retail Sales (CFRS) Facilities 2024 / NFPA 1124]

question: A sprinkler contractor covers newly installed aboveground sprinkler pipe before CCFMO inspection. What enforcement action may CCFMO take per the Pre-Construction Meeting?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office Pre-Construction Meeting (item 18), the sprinkler contractor must not cover any sprinkler pipe prior to approval by CCFMO. If the contractor fails to comply, they will be required to remove the product covering the pipe. Similarly, per item 14, underground pipe that is covered without CCFMO approval must be fully exposed for inspection. The same installing contractor is responsible for scheduling the inspection, and inspection times are limited — the contractor must be prepared for inspection when it is scheduled. [TYPE: reasoning | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting]

question: A building with an occupant load of 750 has only two exits. Is this code-compliant per NFPA 101, and what is required to achieve compliance?
ground_truth: No, this is not compliant. Per NFPA 101 Section 7.4.1.2, as referenced in the Cobb County New Building Plan Review checklist (item 12), occupant loads between 500 and 1,000 require a minimum of 3 exits. A building with 750 occupants and only 2 exits is deficient by one exit. The exits must also be separated by not less than 1/2 the diagonal distance of the area served in a non-sprinklered building (NFPA 101 7.5.1.3.2), or not less than 1/3 the diagonal distance in a sprinklered building (7.5.1.3.3). [TYPE: reasoning | SOURCE: New Shell Building Plan Review 2024 / NFPA 101 Sec. 7.4.1.2]

question: A fire inspector arrives for a welded pipe check and finds no welder's mark on the pipe and no welder's certificate on site. What must occur before the inspection can proceed per the CCFMO Pre-Construction Meeting?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office Pre-Construction Meeting (item 21), pipe checks are required for welded pipe only. The welder's mark must appear on the pipe OR the welder's certificate must be provided prior to the inspection. The pipe must be unbundled with outlets facing up. If neither the welder's mark nor the certificate is available on site at the time of inspection, the inspection cannot proceed. The contractor must obtain and present one of these two forms of verification before rescheduling. [TYPE: reasoning | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting]

question: Can a portable generator at a Consumer Fireworks Retail Sales facility use Class I combustible liquid fuel? What fuel is permitted and how close may the generator be located?
ground_truth: No, a CFRS generator cannot use Class I combustible liquid fuel as the primary fuel. Per NFPA 1124 Section 7.4.9.2.1 (checklist item 30), generators shall only use Class II or Class III fuel. Class II and Class III combustible liquid generator fuel is limited to not more than 5 gallons; Class I is limited to 2 gallons (checklist item 29, NFPA 1124 7.3.18). Generators and their fuel must be located at least 20 feet from the CFRS facility (item 31, NFPA 1124 7.4.9.2.2). Unlimited fuel is allowed only where stored at least 50 feet from the CFRS facility (item 32, NFPA 1124 7.4.9.2.4). [TYPE: reasoning | SOURCE: Consumer Fireworks Retail Sales (CFRS) Facilities 2024 / NFPA 1124]

question: A developer wants to conduct blasting at 2:00 p.m. on a Saturday. Is this permitted under a standard Cobb County development blasting permit?
ground_truth: No, this is not permitted. Per Cobb County Code Sec. 18-361(d)(2), permitted hours of blasting activity are 10:00 a.m. through 4:00 p.m., Monday through Friday only. No blasting is permitted on weekends, state holidays, or federal holidays. Although 2:00 p.m. falls within the daily time window, Saturday blasting violates the day-of-week restriction and constitutes a violation of the standard permit conditions. [TYPE: reasoning | SOURCE: Cobb County Development Blasting Ordinance Sec. 18-361]

question: A developer's named blaster becomes unavailable after a blasting permit has been issued. What must the user do before the replacement blaster can detonate?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code Sec. 18-361(d)(3), if the blaster identified in the application changes after the permit is issued, the user must notify the county of the name, address, contact information, and registration requirements of the replacement blaster prior to any detonation by the replacement blaster. Per condition (7), the permit is issued to the user and is not transferable. The replacement blaster must also meet state registration requirements per Sec. 18-359(a)(2), including holding a Blaster Certification Card per Chapter 120-3-10. [TYPE: reasoning | SOURCE: Cobb County Development Blasting Ordinance Sec. 18-361]

question: An 18,000 CFM HVAC unit is being installed in a building with a fire alarm system. What duct detector placement and reporting requirements apply?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Fire Marshal Office Pre-Construction Meeting (item 43), an HVAC smoke shutdown test is required on units over 2,000 CFM. Because this unit exceeds 15,000 CFM, duct detectors are required on both the supply and return sides. Because the building has a fire alarm system, the HVAC shutdown must report as a supervisory signal to the fire alarm system. Live smoke must be used for the shutdown test. The duct detector must not be omitted from either side simply because the building has a fire alarm system. [TYPE: reasoning | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting]

question: Combining the Existing Apartment Occupancy Checklist and NFPA 25, what are all the required testing and inspection frequencies for fire sprinkler systems at existing apartment occupancies in Cobb County?
ground_truth: From the Cobb County Existing Apartment Occupancy Checklist and supporting standards combined: (1) Annual fire sprinkler inspection and testing per NFPA 25; (2) Semi-annual waterflow test records must be maintained and current; (3) Spare sprinkler heads — minimum six — must be available on premises at all times per NFPA 25 Sec. 5.4.1.4; (4) Special sprinkler wrench — one per type of sprinkler installed — must be available per NFPA 25 Sec. 5.4.1.6; (5) FDC must be visible with appropriate signage and must be accessible; (6) All sprinkler valves must be supervised; (7) Riser room signage must be in place. Private fire hydrants at apartment occupancies require annual general maintenance and a flow test every 3 years per Cobb County Ordinance Sec. 54-59. [TYPE: multi-context | SOURCE: Existing Apartment Checklist + Common Code Violations + Cobb County Hydrant Ordinance Sec. 54-59]

question: Combining the Existing Apartment Occupancy Checklist and Common Code Violations, what electrical violations are inspected at existing apartment occupancies and what code sections govern each?
ground_truth: From the Existing Apartment Occupancy Checklist and Common Code Violations combined: (1) Electrical panel clearance of 30 inches — Section 605.3 of the 2012 IFC (Common Code Violation #6): requires 30-inch wide, 36-inch deep, 78-inch high working space in front of electrical service equipment with no storage. (2) Electrical junction boxes covered — Section 605.6 of the 2012 IFC (Common Code Violation #2): open junction boxes and open wiring splices prohibited; approved covers required for all switch and outlet boxes. (3) Extension cords not in permanent use — Section 605.5 of the 2012 IFC (Common Code Violation #7): extension cords shall not substitute for permanent wiring and shall be used only with portable appliances. [TYPE: multi-context | SOURCE: Existing Apartment Checklist + Common Code Violations / 2012 IFC Sec. 605.3, 605.5, 605.6]

question: Combining Cobb County Hydrant Ordinance Sec. 54-59 and the Private Hydrant Guide, what are the qualifications and record-keeping requirements for companies performing private hydrant maintenance and flow tests?
ground_truth: From Cobb County Ordinance Sec. 54-59 and the Private Hydrant Guide combined: (1) Qualifications: All maintenance and flow tests must be performed by a 'qualified company on file with the Cobb County Fire Marshal's Office.' Companies must register with the CCFMO before conducting private hydrant work. (2) Maintenance records: Per Sec. 54-59(c), maintenance records must be maintained on the premises for inspection by the CCFMO. Per the Private Hydrant Guide, all records must also be uploaded into Mobile-eyes for CCFMO review. (3) Flow test records: Per Sec. 54-59(d), a site map showing flow and residual hydrants numbered appropriately must be provided with each flow test report. Flow testing follows the AWWA M17 manual. (4) Frequency: Annual inspection and maintenance; measured flow test every 3 years. [TYPE: multi-context | SOURCE: Cobb County Hydrant Ordinance Sec. 54-59 + Private Hydrant Guide 2023]

question: Combining the New Shell Building Plan Review, Pre-Construction Meeting, and Common Code Violations, what are all the requirements governing fire department connections (FDCs) for new multi-story commercial buildings in Cobb County?
ground_truth: From the New Shell Building Plan Review, Pre-Construction Meeting, and Common Code Violations combined: (1) Height: FDCs installed at 18 to 48 inches from grade (Pre-Construction Meeting item 24). (2) Hydrant proximity: No more than 100 feet from a hydrant where a standpipe is installed (item 24). (3) Signage: Required on all FDCs (item 20); FDC key plans required if FDC feeds partial coverage. On existing buildings where the FDC is not visible to apparatus, an approved sign with 'FDC' letters at least 6 inches high must be provided (Common Code Violation #4, IFC Sec. 912.2.2). (4) Clear space: 36 inches wide, 36 inches deep, 78 inches high working space in front of wall-mounted FDCs per IFC Sec. 912.3.2. (5) High-rise buildings: Two remotely located FDCs and a hydrant within 100 feet required per NFPA 14 7.12.2 (High-Rise Checklist item 11). [TYPE: multi-context | SOURCE: New Shell Building Plan Review 2024 + Pre-Construction Meeting + Common Code Violations + High-Rise Checklist 2024]

question: Combining the Development Blasting Ordinance Sec. 18-359 and the Blasting Permit Application, what additional studies or assessments beyond the seven standard documents might the Fire Marshal's Office require?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code Sec. 18-359(a)(5) and the Blasting Permit Application (item 9), the director or Fire Marshal may require additional information, which may include: (a) a pre-blast assessment prepared by a geotechnical engineer assessing geology of the blast site and surrounding area out to 2,000 feet, evaluating potential for damage to structures within 2,000 feet; (b) pre-blast inspection of structures near the proposed blast location, including videotaping of structures; (c) bond to protect county facilities; (d) hydrological study; (e) geological study; and (f) test wells. Per the Application (item 8), if blasting occurs within 2,000 feet of structures or facilities, a Pre-Blast assessment must be maintained for reference. [TYPE: multi-context | SOURCE: Cobb County Development Blasting Ordinance Sec. 18-359 + Development Blasting Permit Application 2025]

question: What seven locations must have two-way telephone communication connected to the Emergency Command Center in a high-rise building per NFPA 72 Section 24.5.1.11?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 72 Section 24.5.1.11 as referenced in the Cobb County High-Rise Building Plan Review checklist (item 13), two-way telephone communication to the Emergency Command Center is required from: (a) elevator cabs [24.5.1.11(3)], (b) elevator lobbies [24.5.1.11(4)], (c) elevator machine rooms [24.5.1.11(5)], (d) standby power room(s) [24.5.1.11(6)], (e) fire pump room(s) [24.5.1.11(7)], (f) area(s) of refuge [24.5.1.11(8)], and (g) each floor level of exit stairs [24.5.1.11(9)]. The fire alarm system must use emergency voice/alarm communication per NFPA 101 11.8.4.1. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: High-Rise Checklist 2024 / NFPA 72 Sec. 24.5.1.11]

question: What fire wall ratings and opening protection requirements does NFPA 1124 impose on CFRS facilities based on distance from adjacent buildings per Table 7.4.7.1.1?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 1124 Table 7.4.7.1.1 as referenced in the Cobb County CFRS Facilities Plan Review checklist (item 3): (a) Adjacent buildings less than 10 feet away require 2-hour rated fire wall separations with 90-minute opening protection; (b) Adjacent buildings 10 feet to less than 60 feet away require 1-hour rated fire wall separation with 45-minute opening protection; (c) Adjacent buildings 60 feet or more away require no fire ratings. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Consumer Fireworks Retail Sales (CFRS) Facilities 2024 / NFPA 1124 Table 7.4.7.1.1]

question: What fire pump testing and logging requirements must existing apartment occupancies comply with per the Cobb County Existing Apartment Occupancy Checklist?
ground_truth: Per the Cobb County Existing Apartment Occupancy Checklist, fire pump compliance requires: (1) an annual fire pump test — records must be current and available for inspection; and (2) a weekly pump run log must be maintained and available on site. These requirements derive from NFPA 25 standards for fire pump inspection, testing, and maintenance. Deficiency in either document will result in a failed inspection item. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Existing Apartment Checklist]

question: What exit sign placement requirement specifies maximum spacing in exit access corridors per NFPA 101, as used in the Cobb County New Shell Building Plan Review?
ground_truth: Per NFPA 101 Section 7.10.1.5.2 as referenced in the Cobb County New Building Plan Review checklist (items 9a and 29), exit signs must be placed such that no point in an exit access corridor is in excess of the rated viewing distance or 100 feet (30 meters), whichever is less, from the nearest sign. Exit signs must be provided as required in the applicable occupancy chapter. Egress illumination must be provided per Section 7.8.1, and emergency lighting per Section 7.9.1. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: New Shell Building Plan Review 2024 / NFPA 101 Sec. 7.10.1.5.2]

question: After the CCFMO approves plans for a tenant space in unincorporated Cobb County, what are the next steps to request FMO inspections?
ground_truth: Per the 2026 Cobb County Tenant Space CO Procedures: after FMO approves the plans, they are uploaded into Accela for review by other applicable departments. The applicant receives a gold-colored instructional sheet added to the approved plans, which must be followed along with any stamps or stickers on the plans. To request FMO inspections, the applicant must log into the active job in the Mobile-eyes system at cobbcounty.gov/fmo (Certificate of Occupancy section), click the Request Inspections button, and follow the directions. Building department inspections are requested from Cobb County Building Department at 770-528-2073. All inspections must pass before the Certificate of Occupancy can be requested. [TYPE: reasoning | SOURCE: Tenant CO Procedures 2026]

question: Combining the Pre-Construction Meeting, Cobb County Hydrant Ordinance Sec. 54-59, and the Private Hydrant Guide, what are all the commissioning, orientation, color, marking, and clearance requirements for new private fire hydrants at commercial construction sites?
ground_truth: From the Pre-Construction Meeting, Cobb County Ordinance Sec. 54-59, and Private Hydrant Guide combined: (1) Timing: All private hydrants must be commissioned before combustible materials are brought on site (Pre-Construction item 12). (2) Flush and flow test: A complete flush of all underground piping must be witnessed by CCFMO; a flow test is required for all new hydrants with testing certificates per NFPA 24 (2013) Figure 10.10.1 (item 16). (3) Orientation and outlet height: Hydrants must be oriented properly with a minimum of 18 inches in height to the center of the large-diameter outlet (item 15). (4) Color and marking: Painted silver; orange reflective tape (minimum 1-inch wide, CCFMO-approved) wrapped around the entire bonnet circumference, ends overlapping at least 1 inch; public hydrants use white tape (Ordinance Sec. 54-59(b)). (5) Circumference clearance: Minimum 36-inch clearance around the hydrant with no vegetation blocking the view (item 15). (6) Vertical clearance: 6 feet 8 inches from top of hydrant to any tree limbs, hedges, or bushes (Private Hydrant Guide). (7) Ongoing: Annual maintenance and 3-year flow test by a qualified CCFMO-registered company; records maintained on premises and uploaded to Mobile-eyes. [TYPE: multi-context | SOURCE: Pre-Construction Meeting + Cobb County Hydrant Ordinance Sec. 54-59 + Private Hydrant Guide 2023]

question: What is the minimum fine per day for continuing work after receiving a stop work order under Cobb County Code Sec. 54-52?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-52, the county amends International Fire Code Section 111.4 to read as follows: any person who shall continue any work after having been served with a stop work order, except such work as that person is directed to perform to remove a violation or unsafe condition, shall be liable to a fine of not less than $1,000.00 per day. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-52]

question: What is the fee schedule for silencing or resetting a fire alarm without prior approval in Cobb County per Code Sec. 54-103?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-103(b), fees for silencing or resetting a fire alarm without prior approval in a consecutive 12-month period are: First violation = $150.00 minimum; Second violation = $250.00 minimum; Third violation = $500.00 minimum; Fourth violation = not more than $1,000.00. Fees are assessed by the fire marshal within three months of the incident. Payment is due within 30 days; unpaid amounts bear interest at 1.5 percent per month. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-103]

question: What is the false fire alarm fee structure in Cobb County per Code Sec. 54-103, and how many alarms are allowed before fees begin?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-103(c), false alarm fees in a consecutive 12-month period are: First two false alarms = no fee; Third false alarm = minimum $150.00; Fourth false alarm = minimum $250.00; Fifth and any subsequent false alarm = not less than $500.00 nor more than $1,000.00. Fees are assessed within three months of the incident, due within 30 days, with 1.5 percent per month interest on unpaid balances. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-103]

question: What setback distance from structures is required for recreational fires in unincorporated Cobb County per Sec. 54-116, and what are the permitted burning hours?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-116(a), recreational fires shall not be located within 25 feet of a structure or other combustible material. Conditions that could cause the fire to spread within 25 feet of a structure must be eliminated prior to ignition. Burning is permitted between 10:00 a.m. and 10:30 p.m. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-116]

question: What must be kept within 25 feet of any outdoor fire in unincorporated Cobb County per Sec. 54-116, and how close must an attending adult remain?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-116(c), any type of outdoor burning must be constantly attended by an adult who must be outdoors within 100 feet of the fire and watching at all times. A water-charged hose that can reach the fire, or a minimum 4-A fire extinguisher, must be on hand and ready for use. The nozzle of the hose or fire extinguisher must be kept within 25 feet of the fire. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-116]

question: What permit is required for a bonfire in Cobb County, what are the minimum setback distances, and what are the maximum dimensions per Sec. 54-117?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-117(a), a bonfire requires a permit from Cobb County Fire and Emergency Services. Minimum setback requirements are 50 feet from a structure or other combustible material and 100 feet from property lines, with a 50-foot clear area around the fire free of combustible materials. Maximum dimensions are 12 feet in each of length, width, and height. Burning is permitted between 10:00 a.m. and 10:30 p.m. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-117]

question: What are the outdoor burning violation penalty tiers under Cobb County Code Sec. 54-118, and over what time period are multiple violations counted?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-118(a), penalties for violations of burning prohibitions or safeguards are counted over a consecutive 36-month period as follows: First violation = written notice; Second violation = summons and $100.00 fine; Third violation = summons and $250.00 fine; Fourth violation = summons and $500.00 fine; Fifth and any subsequent violations = summons, $1,000.00 fine, and/or up to six months in jail. Violations related to land clearing permit conditions start higher: First = $500.00; Second = $1,000.00; Third and subsequent = $1,000.00 and/or six months in jail per Sec. 54-118(b). [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-118]

question: What are the composition, quorum, and voting requirements for the Fire Prevention and Protection Safety Ordinance Board of Appeals per Cobb County Code Sec. 54-119?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-119, the Fire Prevention and Protection Safety Ordinance Board of Appeals consists of five voting members appointed by the Board of Commissioners. Members serve four-year terms; initial appointments were staggered at one, two, and three years before moving to four-year terms. Members must be qualified by experience and training in fire hazards, explosions, hazardous conditions, or fire protection systems, and must not be county employees. Three members constitute a quorum. All decisions require affirmative votes of a majority, but not less than three members. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-119]

question: What is the timeline for the Board of Appeals to hear a filed appeal, and within how many days must a written decision be issued per Cobb County Code Sec. 54-120 and Sec. 54-121?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-120(c), the Board of Appeals must meet within 10 days of the filing of an appeal, or at the next regularly scheduled meeting. Appeals must be filed at least 10 days before the next scheduled meeting; otherwise a specially called meeting is set by the chairperson. Per Sec. 54-121(c), decisions must be provided in writing to the fire marshal within three days of the hearing. A certified copy is furnished to the appellant and to the fire marshal. Per Sec. 54-121(e), all decisions are final and may only be reviewed by certiorari to the superior court. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-120, 54-121]

question: What sprinkler standards apply to new apartment buildings based on number of stories under Cobb County Code Sec. 54-93, and what additional areas must be protected?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-93(b), all new apartment buildings two stories or more in height require supervised fire protection sprinkler systems. NFPA 13R systems are allowed in structures up to and including four stories in height. All buildings above four stories must be protected by an NFPA 13 system. Exception: sprinkler systems are not required for new single-story apartments with exit doors leading directly outside from each dwelling unit. In addition, all balconies and storage closets accessed from the balcony must be protected by the fire protection sprinkler system. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-93]

question: What minimum liability insurance is required for a fire sprinkler permit in Cobb County, and what are the insurer requirements per Code Sec. 54-83?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-83(b), the fire marshal shall not issue any fire sprinkler permit unless the applicant provides proof of comprehensive liability insurance with coverage of not less than $300,000.00. The policy must cover loss to property or personal injury caused by the fire protection sprinkler contractor. The policy must be purchased from an insurer authorized to do business in Georgia. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-83]

question: What is the plan review fee for a storage or fuel tank permit in Cobb County, and what fines apply for failure to obtain a permit or call for inspection per Code Sec. 54-54.1?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-54.1(c), the plan review fee is $300.00 per permit regardless of the number of tanks included. The fine for failure to obtain a permit is $1,000.00. The fine for failure to call for an inspection is $500.00. All tanks are permitted through the Cobb County Fire Marshal's Office. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-54.1]

question: What rules govern temporary tank installations in Cobb County per Code Sec. 54-54.1, including permit requirements and minimum distance from structures?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-54.1(c)(2), temporary tanks — those in use for 90 days or less — do not require a permit. However, temporary tanks must be located no closer than 100 feet from any structure or building and must comply with all other applicable code requirements. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-54.1]

question: When is a Fire Marshal permit required for outdoor social gatherings in Cobb County, and how is crowd size calculated per Code Sec. 54-54.2?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-54.2(c), outdoor social gatherings with projected crowds exceeding 1,000 persons require a permit from the Fire Marshal's Office. The permit may require EMS services, hydration stations, fire watch, or crowd management measures based on fire and life safety assessment. Crowd calculations are estimated at one time — not cumulatively throughout the event. Fees for required special fire services are assessed in accordance with Sec. 54-62. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-54.2]

question: What colors must fire sprinkler control valves, PIVs, WPIVs, and underground fire main sectional control valves be painted per Cobb County Code Sec. 54-96?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-96(a), all control valves for fire sprinkler systems — including PIVs (Post Indicator Valves) and WPIVs (Wall Post Indicator Valves) — shall be painted bright red. Paint shall not be faded, chipped, or rusted through. Indicating sectional control valves for underground fire mains shall be painted light blue. Paint shall not be faded, chipped, or rusted through. Valves in roadway boxes or pits are not required to be painted. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-96]

question: How is 'substantial renovation' defined for purposes of Cobb County Certificate of Occupancy requirements per Code Sec. 54-51?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-51(i), substantial renovation means any construction project involving exits or internal features of a building, or any structure costing more than the building's or structure's assessed value according to county tax records at the time of such renovation. Substantial renovation triggers the requirement to meet the code edition in effect at the time plans are approved, as if new construction per Sec. 54-51(e). [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-51]

question: When a business changes ownership in unincorporated Cobb County with no change in occupancy or renovation, what administrative step is required and will a new CO be issued per Code Sec. 54-51?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-51(g), where there is no change in the classification of occupancy, substantial renovation, reconstruction, renovation, or addition — merely a change in owner or business name — the new owner and/or occupant must complete a 'Notice of Information Change Form' and submit it to the Cobb County Fire Marshal's Office. A new certificate of occupancy will NOT be issued for properties in unincorporated Cobb County or the City of Acworth. A new certificate of occupancy WILL be issued for properties in the city limits of Kennesaw or Powder Springs. This provision does not apply to assembly, storage, or industrial occupancies. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-51]

question: What NICET certification requirements apply to fire alarm plan submittals and on-site work in Cobb County, and what equivalent certification is accepted per Code Sec. 54-101?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-101(d)-(h): Effective July 1, 2015, at least one NICET-certified individual is required on each fire alarm job site. Effective July 1, 2016, all plan submittals must be made only by NICET-certified persons, and all documentation submitted to the fire marshal (record of completion forms, inspection and testing forms, permit applications, etc.) must be completed and signed by NICET-certified persons. Proof of certification must be stamped on plans or accompany submittals. The Electronic Security Association (ESA) National Training School (NTS) Certified Fire Alarm Technician (CFAT) Level II is an approved equivalent to NICET certification. [TYPE: simple | SOURCE: Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-101]

question: What board and care and community living arrangement sprinkler requirements apply under Cobb County Code Sec. 54-93?
ground_truth: Per Cobb County Code of Ordinances Sec. 54-93(e), all new board and care occupancies (personal care homes) with four or more clients, and all community living arrangements, must be equipped with an approved sprinkler system.
